items.prompt_md: Why does the opposite-ends loop use `while lo < hi` instead of `while lo <= hi`?
options: Because once `lo` equals `hi`, the pointers sit on the same single middle element, which trivially matches itself, so there's nothing left to check
Because using `<=` would cause an infinite loop
Because the array might be empty
Because `lo` must always be less than `hi` at the very start of the array
items.answer: Because once `lo` equals `hi`, the pointers sit on the same single middle element, which trivially matches itself, so there's nothing left to check
items.explanation_md: When `lo == hi` there is exactly one leftover element in the middle (odd-length sequence). A single element is always symmetric with itself, so continuing to compare `s[lo]` against `s[hi]` would just check a value against itself — wasted work, not a bug, but the loop is written to stop there because every real pair has already been checked.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the opposite-ends palindrome check shown in the lesson?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each step of the loop retires exactly one pair of positions and `lo`/`hi` move strictly toward each other, so the loop runs at most n/2 times — linear in the length of the sequence.

items.prompt_md: How does the extra space used by the opposite-ends approach compare to building a reversed copy and comparing it to the original?
options: O(1) extra space for opposite-ends vs O(n) extra space for the reversed copy
O(n) extra space for opposite-ends vs O(1) extra space for the reversed copy
Both approaches use O(n) extra space
Both approaches use O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(1) extra space for opposite-ends vs O(n) extra space for the reversed copy
items.explanation_md: The opposite-ends walk only ever needs two integer indices, `lo` and `hi`. Building a reversed copy first means allocating a whole second sequence, which costs O(n) extra memory that the pointer approach avoids entirely.

items.prompt_md: In the container-with-most-water problem, the pointers sit at heights `h[lo]` and `h[hi]` where `h[lo] < h[hi]`. Which pointer should move inward next?
options: `lo` — the pointer at the shorter wall
`hi` — the pointer at the taller wall
Whichever pointer happens to be closer to the middle index
Both pointers should move on every step
items.answer: `lo` — the pointer at the shorter wall
items.explanation_md: The area is capped by the shorter of the two walls. Moving the taller wall inward only shrinks the width while the cap stays the same or gets worse, so it can never beat the current area. Moving the shorter wall is the only move that has a chance of finding a taller wall and improving the result.

items.prompt_md: Why is it safe to move only the shorter-wall pointer in container-with-most-water instead of moving the taller one too?
options: Because moving the taller pointer can never produce a larger area than what's already been recorded — the width shrinks and the height is still capped by the shorter wall
Because the taller wall is always part of the optimal answer
Because moving both pointers on every step is required for the algorithm to terminate
Because the array must be sorted by height first
items.answer: Because moving the taller pointer can never produce a larger area than what's already been recorded — the width shrinks and the height is still capped by the shorter wall
items.explanation_md: Any area with the taller wall as one boundary and a narrower width than the current span is strictly worse, since the height cap doesn't improve and the width only decreases. So keeping the taller wall fixed and moving the shorter one is the only move that can possibly beat the current best.

items.prompt_md: A learner writes a palindrome check but advances `hi` on every iteration even while an inner loop is still skipping punctuation at `lo`. What bug does this introduce?
options: It desynchronizes which characters are actually being compared, so the code may compare the wrong pair of positions and reach a wrong answer
It causes an infinite loop that never terminates
It has no effect — skipping order between `lo` and `hi` never matters
It changes the time complexity from O(n) to O(n^2)
items.answer: It desynchronizes which characters are actually being compared, so the code may compare the wrong pair of positions and reach a wrong answer
items.explanation_md: The two skip-loops (one for `lo`, one for `hi`) exist precisely so each pointer lands on the next real alphanumeric character before the comparison happens. Advancing `hi` unconditionally can walk it past a character `lo` hasn't caught up to yet, so the comparison checks a pair that was never actually mirrored.

items.prompt_md: Which of these is a common off-by-one bug at the point where the pointers meet or cross in an opposite-ends loop?
options: Comparing `s[lo]` and `s[hi]` one extra time after `lo` has already passed `hi`, checking a pair that's out of the valid range or already implicitly validated
Forgetting to initialize `hi` to `len(s) - 1`
Never incrementing `lo` inside the loop
Starting `hi` at index 0 instead of the last index
items.answer: Comparing `s[lo]` and `s[hi]` one extra time after `lo` has already passed `hi`, checking a pair that's out of the valid range or already implicitly validated
items.explanation_md: If the loop condition or an inner adjustment lets a comparison run after `lo` and `hi` have already crossed, you're rechecking (or misreading) positions that no longer form a real unchecked pair — a classic off-by-one at the termination boundary.

items.prompt_md: Which condition must the input satisfy for an opposite-ends scan to correctly find a target-sum pair (as opposed to a pure symmetry check like a palindrome)?
options: The array must be sorted, so that moving `lo` forward always increases the running sum and moving `hi` backward always decreases it
The array must contain no duplicate values
The array must be sorted in descending order specifically
The array's length must be even
items.answer: The array must be sorted, so that moving `lo` forward always increases the running sum and moving `hi` backward always decreases it
items.explanation_md: Deciding which pointer to move for a target-sum search only works because sortedness guarantees a predictable direction of change: advancing `lo` can only raise the sum, retreating `hi` can only lower it. Without that order, moving a pointer doesn't reliably move you toward or away from the target.

items.prompt_md: For a palindrome check specifically (not a sorted target-sum search), does the opposite-ends approach require the input to be sorted?
options: No — palindrome checking only compares mirrored positions against each other, so sortedness is irrelevant
Yes, the string must be sorted alphabetically first
Yes, but only for strings longer than 10 characters
No, but only if the string has an even number of characters
items.answer: No — palindrome checking only compares mirrored positions against each other, so sortedness is irrelevant
items.explanation_md: Sortedness matters when the pointer movement decision depends on comparing values to a target (like a sum). A symmetry check like palindrome only cares whether mirrored characters match, which has nothing to do with any global ordering of the sequence.

items.prompt_md: If you checked every pair of positions with nested loops instead of using opposite-ends pointers for a palindrome-style symmetric condition, what time complexity would you get?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Nested loops that compare every pair of positions do roughly n * n work in the worst case, even though only the n/2 mirrored pairs actually matter — the opposite-ends walk gets the same correctness in linear time by only ever checking the pairs that could break the property.

items.prompt_md: What is the key invariant that lets each opposite-ends step permanently "retire" the pair of positions it just checked?
options: The pair currently at `lo` and `hi` is the only pair left that could still break the property being tested — once it's checked, it can never need re-checking
Every element in the sequence must be visited exactly twice before the loop can end
The pointers always start at index 0 and increase together
The array's size doubles on each iteration of the loop
items.answer: The pair currently at `lo` and `hi` is the only pair left that could still break the property being tested — once it's checked, it can never need re-checking
items.explanation_md: Because `lo` and `hi` only ever move toward each other, every pair the algorithm skips past has already been implicitly ruled out or confirmed. The pair at the current extremes is the sole remaining candidate that hasn't been checked, which is exactly why one comparison per step is enough.

items.prompt_md: Which of these problems fits the opposite-ends trigger — "the condition only depends on the pair currently at the extremes"?
options: Checking whether a string is a palindrome
Counting how many times each character appears in a string
Finding the length of the longest substring without repeating characters
Grouping a list of strings into sets of anagrams
items.answer: Checking whether a string is a palindrome
items.explanation_md: A palindrome check is the textbook case: whether the property holds depends only on the pair of characters currently at the two extremes, never on anything in the middle, which is exactly the shape opposite-ends pointers are built for.

items.prompt_md: You have a sorted array of distinct integers and need to determine whether any two of them add up exactly to a target sum.
options: Brute-force every pair
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Slide a growing/shrinking window
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: The array is sorted, so moving the low pointer up strictly increases the sum and moving the high pointer down strictly decreases it — a direct converging-pointer search.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and must return the original indices of the two numbers that add up to a target sum.
options: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Brute-force every pair
Slide a growing/shrinking window
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
items.explanation_md: Sorting would destroy the original indices you're required to return, and there's no sortedness to exploit anyway. A hash map recording value-to-index lets you find a complement in one linear pass without losing index information.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a given string reads the same forwards and backwards after stripping out spaces and punctuation.
options: Slide a growing/shrinking window
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Brute-force every pair
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: The condition only depends on the mirrored pair of characters at the current extremes, the textbook opposite-ends symmetry check.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of wall heights and must find the two walls that, together with the ground between them, trap the most water.
options: Brute-force every pair
Slide a growing/shrinking window
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: Starting pointers at both ends and always advancing the shorter wall explores every candidate answer in one linear pass, without needing to check every pair explicitly.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse an array of characters in place, without allocating a second array.
options: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Brute-force every pair
Slide a growing/shrinking window
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: Swapping the elements at `lo` and `hi` and stepping them inward reverses the array in O(1) extra space — exactly what opposite-ends pointers are for.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning a list of integers, in no particular order, and need to know as fast as possible whether any value appears more than once.
options: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Brute-force every pair
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Slide a growing/shrinking window
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
items.explanation_md: Duplicates can be anywhere and the array isn't guaranteed sorted, so there's no meaningful "extremes" to converge from. A seen-set catches a repeat the moment it's scanned in one linear pass.

items.prompt_md: You have a sorted array and need to find the pair of numbers whose sum is closest to a given target, narrowing in from both ends.
options: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Slide a growing/shrinking window
Brute-force every pair
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: Sortedness lets you tell, at each step, whether moving the low or high pointer moves the running sum closer to the target — a direct converging-pointer search.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether one string is an anagram of another — made of exactly the same letters, in any order.
options: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Slide a growing/shrinking window
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Brute-force every pair
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
items.explanation_md: Anagram-checking compares letter frequencies, not mirrored positions — order doesn't matter at all, so a hash map of counts is the right tool, not converging pointers.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of integers, decide whether it contains two elements whose sum is exactly zero.
options: Slide a growing/shrinking window
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Brute-force every pair
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: This is a sorted target-sum search with target zero — move the low pointer up when the sum is too negative, the high pointer down when it's too positive.

items.prompt_md: You're checking whether a sequence of moves is a mirror image of itself — the first move should cancel the last, the second should cancel the second-to-last, and so on inward.
options: Brute-force every pair
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Slide a growing/shrinking window
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: "Cancels with the mirrored move at the other end" is exactly the opposite-ends symmetry trigger — check the pair at the current extremes and converge inward.

items.prompt_md: Given an array already sorted in non-decreasing order, you need to find out whether two of its elements multiply to a given target product.
options: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Brute-force every pair
Slide a growing/shrinking window
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: Sortedness again gives a predictable direction: for non-negative values, advancing the low pointer increases the product and retreating the high pointer decreases it, same shape as the sum version.

items.prompt_md: You need to verify that a sequence of brackets is symmetric — the opening bracket at the start of the sequence should match the closing bracket at the end, and so on working inward.
options: Keep a hash map/set of seen values
Brute-force every pair
Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Slide a growing/shrinking window
items.answer: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
items.explanation_md: The condition depends only on the character pair currently at the extremes, matching front against back — the opposite-ends convergence pattern, not a stack-based nesting check.
